stories.title: PGA Championship Round 1 Underway — McIlroy's Grand Slam Bid Begins at Aronimink

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
IT'S MAJOR WEEK.

The 108th PGA Championship is underway at Aronimink Golf Club in Pennsylvania.

McIlroy chasing his 7th major. Scheffler defending. Cameron Young looking for his first.

Best course. Biggest stakes. Let's go. #PGAChampionship

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Rory McIlroy won the 2026 Masters. He's now chasing back-to-back majors at the PGA Championship.

The last player to win the year's first two majors: Jordan Spieth in 2015.

Can Rory do it? The chase is on at Aronimink. #PGAChampionship

facebook.long_form:
Major week is finally here.

The 108th PGA Championship tees off today at Aronimink Golf Club in Newtown Square, Pennsylvania — and the storylines this year are about as rich as they come.

Here's what to watch:

— Rory McIlroy: The 2026 Masters champion arrives seeking back-to-back major titles. He won six majors. He wants seven. And a win this week would make him the first player since Jordan Spieth in 2015 to claim the year's first two majors.

— Scottie Scheffler: Defending champion. Won at Quail Hollow last year by five strokes. Three top-two finishes in 2026 already. Scheffler skipped the Truist Championship specifically to prepare for this week.

— Cameron Young: World No. 3 and arguably the best player alive without a major. Won The Players Championship and the Cadillac Championship at Doral in 2026. Major drought: 0.

Aronimink is a Donald Ross masterpiece from 1926 that last hosted the PGA Championship in 1962, when Gary Player won. It plays to par 70 and has no intermediate rough — you're either on the fairway or in deep fescue. This course demands precision above all else.

Round 1 is underway. Who are you watching?
facebook.image_caption: PGA Championship Round 1 is HERE. McIlroy. Scheffler. Young. Aronimink Golf Club. Let's go. #PGAChampionship

article.headline: PGA Championship Round 1 Begins: McIlroy's Grand Slam Bid, Scheffler's Defense, and the Aronimink Challenge

stories.title: Lucas Herbert Wins LIV Virginia Wire-to-Wire, Earns US Open Spot

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text:
Lucas Herbert won LIV Golf Virginia at 24-under par — wire-to-wire.

He was battling the flu all week. He opened 64-63 to build an 8-shot lead. Closed 69. Won by 4.

Prize: $4 million. Bonus: a spot in the U.S. Open at Shinnecock Hills. #LIVGolf

facebook.long_form:
Lucas Herbert probably should not have been able to play last week. He was sick with the flu.

He played anyway. He opened 64-63 at Trump National Golf Club in Virginia, built an eight-shot lead, and never looked back. Herbert won LIV Golf Virginia wire-to-wire at 24-under par, finishing four shots clear of Sergio Garcia. His closing 69 — while still fighting illness — was enough.

The prize: $4 million.

The bonus: a spot in the US Open at Shinnecock Hills.

Herbert's victory moved him to third in the LIV Golf Individual Championship standings for 2026, making him the highest-ranked LIV player who was not already exempt from the US Open. The slot opened up accordingly.

4Aces GC also won the team competition in a dramatic playoff, with Anthony Kim shooting a bogey-free 10-under 62 in the final round to help power the team. Kim — one of the more compelling comeback stories in golf — was extraordinary down the stretch.

Herbert is now also in the field at this week's PGA Championship at Aronimink. From sick to US Open exempt in one week. What a sport.
facebook.image_caption: Lucas Herbert. Flu. 24-under. Wire-to-wire win. $4 million. US Open spot. The man just delivered. #LIVGolf

article.headline: Lucas Herbert Wins LIV Virginia Wire-to-Wire, Earns US Open Spot Despite Battling the Flu

stories.title: Nelly Korda's Hall of Fame Countdown — Round 1 at Queen City Today

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Nelly Korda is 4 LPGA Hall of Fame points away.

She's at 23/27 — and the Kroger Queen City Championship starts TODAY in Cincinnati.

A win = 24/27. One step closer to one of golf's most exclusive clubs. #LPGA #QueenCity

facebook.long_form:
The greatest active women's golfer in the world is four points away from golf's most exclusive club.

Nelly Korda sits at 23 of 27 required LPGA Hall of Fame points. She has won three times already in 2026 — at the Tournament of Champions, the Chevron Championship, and the Riviera Maya Classic. Each point brings her closer to a milestone that most professional golfers never reach.

Today, the Kroger Queen City Championship begins at Maketewah Country Club in Cincinnati, Ohio. A win gives Korda 24/27. A win also continues a 2026 season that has already established her as the dominant force in women's golf.

World No. 1 Jeeno Thitikul — fresh off her Mizuho Americas Open win last week — is also in the Cincinnati field, ensuring that whatever happens at the top of the leaderboard this week, it matters. The Queen City Championship runs May 14-17.

Four points. This milestone is close. Are you following along?
facebook.image_caption: Korda is 4 LPGA Hall of Fame points away. Queen City Championship starts TODAY in Cincinnati. Watch closely. #LPGA

article.headline: Korda Is Four Points From the LPGA Hall of Fame — And Queen City Championship Starts Today

stories.title: Aronimink Golf Club — The Course That Will Separate the Field at the 2026 PGA Championship

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
At Aronimink, there is no in-between.

You're on the fairway (3/8 inch cut) or you're in 3.5-inch fescue.

Average green: 8,100 square feet. Last hosted the PGA Championship in 1962.

This course will separate the field fast. #PGAChampionship #Aronimink

facebook.long_form:
Not all major championship courses are built the same. Aronimink Golf Club is a different kind of test.

The Donald Ross design from 1926-1928 — located in the western suburbs of Philadelphia, just 13 miles from where America was founded — last hosted the PGA Championship in 1962. Gary Player won it. This year, Rory McIlroy, Scottie Scheffler, Cameron Young, and 151 other players will find out what made Player's achievement special.

Here's what the course demands:

**The rough** — There is no intermediate cut. You are in the fairway (cut to 3/8 of an inch) or you are in 3.5-inch fescue and bluegrass that can swallow a golf ball and a scorecard together. This is not a course that allows for "bomb-and-gouge" golf. Accuracy off the tee is essential.

**The greens** — They average 8,100 square feet, roughly a third larger than a typical Donald Ross green. They slope boldly and punish shots hit to the wrong quadrant. Position, not just proximity, defines success on these surfaces.

**The bunkers** — 75 of them, placed strategically by Ross to collect wayward approaches and demand precise sand play.

This championship also carries special historical significance: it coincides with America's 250th anniversary, taking place just miles from Philadelphia, where the nation's founding documents were signed in 1776.

Aronimink is ready. Who do you think wins this week?
facebook.image_caption: No intermediate rough. Greens averaging 8,100 sq ft. 75 bunkers. Aronimink will be unforgiving. #PGAChampionship

article.headline: No Intermediate Rough, Massive Greens, 75 Bunkers: Why Aronimink Will Sort Out the 2026 PGA Championship Field Fast

stories.title: McIlroy's Grand Slam Bid in Historical Context — What Winning Back-to-Back Majors Takes

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
If Rory McIlroy wins the PGA Championship this week, he'll have 7 majors — passing Arnold Palmer.

He's already the 2026 Masters champion.

The Grand Slam has never been won in a single calendar year. Could 2026 be the year? #PGAChampionship #RoryMcIlroy

facebook.long_form:
What Rory McIlroy is trying to do this week has barely been done before.

He won the 2026 Masters. He now arrives at the PGA Championship — the second major of the year — as the defending major champion. If he wins this week, he will become the first player since Jordan Spieth in 2015 to claim the first two major championships of the calendar year.

That's a rare enough achievement. But the larger narrative goes even further.

A win at Aronimink would give McIlroy seven career major titles, moving him past Arnold Palmer on the all-time list. The top of that leaderboard — Jack Nicklaus (18), Tiger Woods (15), Walter Hagen (11), Ben Hogan (9), Gary Player (9) — would become a more distant, but more imaginable, conversation.

Then there is the Grand Slam: winning all four major championships in a single calendar year. No player has ever done it in the calendar-year sense. Tiger Woods won four consecutive majors across 2000-2001 — called the "Tiger Slam" — but never within a single calendar year. Rory would need to win here, then the US Open at Shinnecock Hills, and then the Open Championship at Royal St. Andrews. No player in history has cleared that bar.

For now, the focus is Aronimink and Round 1. The rest of the story gets written over four days.

Drop your prediction for the 2026 PGA Championship winner below.
facebook.image_caption: McIlroy won the Masters. He wants the PGA Championship next. Win = 7 majors. Grand Slam still alive. #RoryMcIlroy #PGAChampionship

article.headline: McIlroy's Grand Slam Bid: What History Says About Winning Back-to-Back Majors
